name: Curupira
description: The Curupira is a mythical creature from Brazilian folklore, known primarily as a guardian of the forest. It is often depicted as a small, humanoid being with fiery red or orange hair, backward-facing feet, and sometimes green or blue skin. The backward feet are its most distinctive feature, used to confuse travelers and hunters, leading them astray deeper into the woods. The Curupira is mischievous and enjoys playing pranks on those who disrespect the forest, often leading them in circles or causing them to lose their way. It is said to be incredibly fast and agile, capable of moving silently through the dense undergrowth. Some stories portray the Curupira as a benevolent protector of animals and trees, while others depict it as a dangerous trickster who punishes those who hunt excessively or damage the environment. To appease the Curupira, hunters sometimes leave offerings of tobacco or food in the forest. The creature's legend serves as a cautionary tale about respecting nature and the consequences of greed and disrespect for the environment. Its image is deeply ingrained in Brazilian culture, often used in environmental awareness campaigns.
subtitle: Forest Guardian and Trickster
appearance: The Curupira is typically described as a small humanoid figure, often resembling a child, with bright red or orange hair and backward-facing feet. Its skin color can vary, ranging from brown to green or blue. Some accounts mention large, pointed ears and sharp teeth. Its backward feet are its most recognizable feature, designed to confuse pursuers by making them follow the wrong trail. The Curupira is usually naked or wears simple clothing made of leaves and vines.
habitat: The Curupira inhabits the dense forests and rainforests of Brazil, particularly the Amazon rainforest. It prefers areas with thick vegetation, where it can easily hide and move unseen.
behavior: The Curupira is known for its mischievous and playful nature. It enjoys tricking travelers and hunters, leading them astray with its backward footprints and confusing illusions. It can be benevolent towards those who respect the forest, but it is ruthless towards those who harm it. It is said to be very protective of animals and plants and will actively defend them against poachers and loggers. The Curupira often communicates through whistles and animal sounds, further disorienting those who venture too deep into its territory.
weaknesses: Vine knots (can be trapped by knotted vines)

keyMyths.title: The Backward Footprints
keyMyths.description: The Curupira's backward feet are its most famous trait, used to confuse hunters and travelers. The footprints lead pursuers in the wrong direction, deeper into the forest, making it nearly impossible to track the creature.

keyMyths.title: Guardian of the Forest
keyMyths.description: The Curupira acts as a guardian of the forest, protecting animals and plants from harm. It punishes those who disrespect the environment, leading them astray or causing them to get lost.

sources.author: Cascudo, Luís da Câmara
sources.description: Comprehensive dictionary of Brazilian folklore, including detailed information on the Curupira.
sources.text: Comprehensive dictionary of Brazilian folklore, including detailed information on the Curupira.
